title: Twin-Twin Transfusion Syndrome
chief_complaint: I'm 20 weeks pregnant with twins. My ultrasound showed that one baby has too much fluid and the other has almost none. My belly is getting big really fast and I'm having trouble breathing.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Fundal height 28cm (large for dates - polyhydramnios). Uterus tense. Difficulty palpating fetal parts.
physical_exam.Lungs: Mild dyspnea from uterine distension (diaphragm elevation).
physical_exam.Cervix: Cervical length 2.2cm on US (shortened - risk of preterm birth).

hidden_data.ob_us: Monochorionic-diamniotic twin pregnancy. Twin A (recipient): Polyhydramnios (deepest vertical pocket 12cm - normal <8cm). Distended bladder. Cardiomegaly, tricuspid regurgitation, hydrops (scalp edema, ascites). Twin B (donor): Oligohydramnios (DVP 1cm - stuck twin). Small bladder. Dopplers: Absent end-diastolic flow in umbilical artery (AEDF - ominous).
hidden_data.fetal_echo: Twin A: Cardiomegaly, moderate TR, RV dysfunction, small pericardial effusion. Recipient cardiomyopathy from volume overload. Twin B: Small heart, normal function.
hidden_data.cervical_length: Cervical length 2.2cm (shortened - increased risk of preterm birth).

correct_diagnosis: Twin-Twin Transfusion Syndrome (TTTS) - Quintero Stage III at 20 Weeks
key_learning_points: TTTS = unbalanced blood flow through placental vascular anastomoses in monochorionic twins. Donor twin: Hypovolemia → oliguria → oligohydramnios → stuck twin. Recipient twin: Hypervolemia → polyuria → polyhydramnios → heart failure → hydrops. Occurs in 10-15% of monochorionic twins

Quintero staging: I: Poly/oligohydramnios only. II: Donor bladder not visible. III: Abnormal Dopplers (absent/reversed UA flow, reversed ductus venosus a-wave, pulsatile UV). IV: Hydrops in either twin. V: Death of one or both twins. Progression can be rapid (days)

Untreated TTTS: 80-90% perinatal mortality. Recipient: Heart failure, hydrops, death. Donor: Severe IUGR, anemia, death. Survivors: 20-40% risk of neurologic injury (PVL, IVH) from hemodynamic fluctuations

Treatment: FETOSCOPIC LASER PHOTOCOAGULATION of placental anastomoses (gold standard - interrupts blood flow between twins). Performed 16-26 weeks. Selective laser ablation of all communicating vessels. Solomon technique (complete dichorionization) reduces recurrence. Success: 70-90% at least one survivor, 50-60% both survive

Alternatives: Amnioreduction (serial removal of amniotic fluid) - temporizing, does not treat cause. Septostomy (create hole in dividing membrane - equalizes fluid but worsens hemodynamic instability). Selective feticide (if one twin moribund or severe anomaly). Prognosis after laser: PPROM 30%, Preterm birth 50% <32 weeks. Neurodevelopment normal in 85-90% of survivors. Serial surveillance (weekly US, fetal ECHO) after laser. Delivery at 34-36 weeks (or earlier if indicated)
